analogy: Think of "Anatomy of Flowering Plants" through the chapter's own opening images: ANATOMY OF FLOWERING PLANTS 71 You can very easily see the structural similarities and variations in the external morphology of the larger living organism, both plants and animals. Similarly, if we were to study the internal structure, one also finds several similarities as well as... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: ANATOMY OF FLOWERING PLANTS 71, 6.1 THE TISSUE SYSTEM, 6.1.1 Epidermal Tissue System, ANATOMY OF FLOWERING PLANTS. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Anatomy of Flowering Plants is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that aNATOMY OF FLOWERING PLANTS 71 You can very easily see the structural similarities and variations in the external morphology of the larger living organism, both plants and animals.

The text explains that similarly, if we were to study the internal structure, one also finds several similarities as well as differences.

The text explains that this chapter introduces you to the internal structure and functional organisation of higher plants.

The text explains that study of internal structure of plants is called anatomy.

The text explains that plants have cells as the basic unit, cells are organised into tissues and in turn the tissues are organised into organs.

The text explains that different organs in a plant show differences in their internal structure.

The text explains that within angiosperms, the monocots and dicots are also seen to be anatomically different.

The text explains that internal structures also show adaptations to diverse environments.

The text explains that 6.1 THE TISSUE SYSTEM We were discussing types of tissues based on the types of cells present.

The text explains that let us now consider how tissues vary depending on their location in the plant body.

The text explains that their structure and function would also be dependent on location.

The text explains that on the basis of their structure and location, there are three types of tissue systems.

The text explains that these are the epidermal tissue system, the ground or fundamental tissue system and the vascular or conducting tissue system.

The text explains that 6.1.1 Epidermal Tissue System The epidermal tissue system forms the outer-most covering of the whole plant body and comprises epidermal cells, stomata and the epidermal appendages – the trichomes and hairs.

The text explains that the epidermis is the outermost layer of the primary plant body.

The text explains that it is made up of elongated, compactly ANATOMY OF FLOWERING PLANTS CHAPTER 6 6.1 The Tissue System 6.2 Anatomy of Dicotyledonous and Monocotyledonous Plants

The text explains that the root hairs are unicellular elongations of the epidermal cells and help absorb water and minerals from the soil.

The text explains that the trichomes in the shoot system are usually multicellular.

The text explains that 6.1.2 The Ground Tissue System All tissues except epidermis and vascular bundles constitute the ground tissue.

The text explains that stomata are structures present in the epidermis of leaves.

The text explains that stomata regulate the process of transpiration and gaseous exchange.

The text explains that aNATOMY OF FLOWERING PLANTS 73 6.1.3 The Vascular Tissue System The vascular system consists of complex tissues, the phloem and the xylem.The xylem and phloem together constitute vascular bundles (Figure 6.2).

The text explains that 6.2.5 Dorsiventral (Dicotyledonous) Leaf The vertical section of a dorsiventral leaf through the lamina shows three main parts, namely, epidermis, mesophyll and vascular system.

The text explains that vascular system includes vascular bundles, which can be seen in the veins and the midrib.

The text explains that assimilation of food and its storage, transportation of water, minerals and photosynthates, and mechanical support are the main functions of tissues.

The text explains that there are three types of tissue systems – epidermal, ground and vascular.

The text explains that the epidermal tissue systems are made of epidermal cells, stomata and the epidermal appendages.

The text explains that the ground tissue system forms the main bulk of the plant.

The text explains that the vascular tissue system is formed by the xylem and phloem.

The text explains that the vascular bundles form the conducting tissue and translocate water, minerals and food material.

The text explains that monocotyledonous and dicotyledonous plants show marked variation in their internal structures.

The text explains that the transverse section of a plant material shows the following anatomical features - (a) the vascular bundles are conjoint, scattered and surrounded by a sclerenchymatous bundle sheaths.

The text explains that explain the structure of stomata with a labelled diagram.

The text explains that name the three basic tissue systems in the flowering plants.

The text explains that describe the internal structure of a dorsiventral leaf with the help of labelled diagrams.

The text explains that they differ in type, number and location of vascular bundles.

The text explains that the secondary growth occurs in most of the dicotyledonous roots and stems.

The text explains that draw illustrations to bring out the anatomical difference between (a) Monocot root and Dicot root (b) Monocot stem and Dicot stem 2.

The text explains that cut a transverse section of young stem of a plant from your school garden and observe it under the microscope.

The text explains that how would you ascertain whether it is a monocot stem or a dicot stem?

The text explains that how is the study of plant anatomy useful to us?

Source: NCERT Class 11 Biology, Chapter 6: Anatomy of Flowering Plants

concise_notes.definition: ANATOMY OF FLOWERING PLANTS 71 You can very easily see the structural similarities and variations in the external morphology of the larger living organism, both plants and animals.
Plants have cells as the basic unit, cells are organised into tissues and in turn the tissues are organised into organs.

This chapter introduces you to the internal structure and functional organisation of higher plants.
Plants have cells as the basic unit, cells are organised into tissues and in turn the tissues are organised into organs.

prelims_traps: Do not treat ANATOMY OF FLOWERING PLANTS 71 and 6.1 THE TISSUE SYSTEM as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Anatomy of Flowering Plants; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Anatomy of Flowering Plants through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use ANATOMY OF FLOWERING PLANTS 71 and 6.1 THE TISSUE SYSTEM to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
